narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've put into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our predictive model saw your path to the bar exam as riskier than we can support right now—it's not about you as a person, but the patterns it spotted in your profile that echoed too many past struggles. Picture your first-year grades landing you in the bottom decile of your class—that was the biggest signal for the model, like an early warning flare that set the tone for potential hurdles ahead, pushing hardest toward this 63.9% chance of not passing the bar. Your third-year decile matching that same low rank doubled down on the concern, reinforcing a story of consistent challenges in keeping up academically. Layered on top was your LSAT score of 25.5, which the model saw as another piece of a tougher puzzle, not quite bridging the gap to stronger bar passage odds. Even your upper-middle family income, while a solid foundation, didn't shift the needle enough in this mix, as it sometimes aligns with profiles that still falter under pressure. Your Asian background and male gender entered the picture too, carrying subtle influences from historical data that tilted toward risk. Your 3.0 undergrad GPA and full-time study plans rounded out the concerns, painting a full portrait of someone the model worries might face an uphill battle on the bar. It's this combination, not any one thing, that made the model cautious—strong in commitment, but with academic markers that historically predict more failures than successes. The good news? This isn't set in stone; you could retake the LSAT, build those early grades, or seek targeted prep to rewrite your story for the next application. We're rooting for you to prove the model wrong and chase that bar passage with everything you've got. Hang in there.
